title: Repeated storms are turning disaster recovery into a way of life, and leaving communities facing disaster fatigue
content: As storms intensify in our warming world, recovery no longer feels permanent in places at risk of disasters. Instead, it’s too often a temporary reprieve before the next disaster hits. Recognizing community disaster fatigue is an important step toward building resilient recovery systems.
snippet: As storms intensify in our warming world, recovery no longer feels permanent in places at risk of disasters. Instead, it’s too often a temporary reprieve before the next disaster hits. Recognizing
creator: Lee Ann Rawlins Williams
sourceDescription: Insight and inspiration in turbulent times.

title: Introducing PLAN, the tool that finds every public lands fight open for comment right now
content: The Public Lands Action Network is a free tool that finds every public lands decision open for comment and helps you write the one comment the law makes them answer.
snippet: The Public Lands Action Network is a free tool that finds every public lands decision open for comment and helps you write the one comment the law makes them answer.
creator: Will Pattiz

title: On autumn asters, bumblebees find warmth at season’s end
content: Sowing flowers in spring may have a surprising effect on the inner lives of bees.
snippet: Sowing flowers in spring may have a surprising effect on the inner lives of bees.

title: ‘Industrial memories’: What the history of the Anthropocene can tell us about where we’re headed
content: Industrial landscapes are living reminders of how centuries of fossil-fuel development reshaped Earth’s systems. As the Anthropocene unfolds, that legacy continues to shape our future. Historian Sabine Höhler argues that bridging the divide between human and Earth history is essential to understanding where we are and at what cost.

creator: Rosa Leunbach

title: Stop press, same news: No energy transition, again
content: Has the much‑discussed energy transition actually begun? So far, the answer is no. With only brief dips in crisis years, global fossil fuel use has risen every year since 1983. The newly released 2025 data confirm: humanity burned more of every major energy source in 2025 than ever before.

creator: Chris Smaje
sourceDescription: Insight and inspiration in turbulent times.

title: Limestone whitewash: An old material with practical benefits
content: Modern paints contain toxic chemicals that linger in our homes and bodies, while traditional lime-based whitewash offers a low-energy, low-tox alternative that brightens buildings, protects surfaces and can even ‘sweeten’ acidic soils to boost fertility.
snippet: Modern paints contain toxic chemicals that linger in our homes and bodies, while traditional lime-based whitewash offers a low-energy, low-tox alternative that brightens buildings, protects surfaces
creator: Brian Kaller

title: Crazy Town: Episode 129. Finding Crazy Town Part 1: The Strait of Everything
content: What happens when the global economy depends on fish traveling 10,000 miles, oil flowing through a single strait, and enough cheap fossil fuel energy to build civilizations that previous generations couldn't even imagine?

title: Shutting down federal bee labs threatens bees, beekeepers and the US food system
content: The U.S. Department of Agriculture plans to close the Beltsville Agricultural Research Center, a 6,500-acre agricultural research station in Maryland that is home to the nation’s premier bee research and disease diagnosis hub. The closure comes at a critical moment for bees.

title: How propaganda and false information are undermining humanitarian work
content: From vaccine hesitancy to conflict-zone rumors, false information is making it harder for humanitarian organizations to build trust, protect civilians, and save lives.
snippet: From vaccine hesitancy to conflict-zone rumors, false information is making it harder for humanitarian organizations to build trust, protect civilians, and save lives.

title: How US renewable-energy growth persists despite federal policy uncertainty
content: Despite recent shifts in federal energy policies, a Carbon Brief analysis shows that the US transition to renewable energy is continuing.
snippet: Despite recent shifts in federal energy policies, a Carbon Brief analysis shows that the US transition to renewable energy is continuing.
creator: Dhruv Modi

title: Why building inspiring alternatives is necessary to counter authoritarianism
content: Vulnerable communities face growing threats: the climate crisis is outpacing scientists’ worst predictions and authoritarianism is no longer a distant possibility. So what can we do? We build. We shift away from reform and instead direct our energy toward creating entirely new systems.
